item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My husband's name is William, and his childhood was… Well, obviously not this bad, as he's still around, but damn this article struck close to home on multiple levels.

Add my vote to the recommendation that Jr. Researcher Covington be given some sort of award for his work here. At least one iteration of the unending cycle of misery is looking to have a happy outcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a link to the Site-77 hub! I actually just picked a number at random for the site but after reading 77's dossier I think it actually works really well here, so I'll chalk it up to serendipity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was more than a little gut-wrenching, I think you may have been a little heavy-handed with the requests to be made to forget at the end of the interview. The payoff of the parents not being able to take the easy way out is still a good one, but if their attitude was dialed down a tad, or even if their requests to be freed from their burden took a less direct form, it might hit harder.

Also, you should totally link to the Site-77 hub in your containment procedures >.>

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there an ethics committee equivalent of the Foundation Star - exceptional humanity being displayed in the containment or neutralization of an anomaly? Because Junior Researcher Covington has definitely earned at least a banana sticker on his employee folder here.

+1, especially for the amnestics being denied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, they don't deserve it. Permanent containment is basically equivalent to a life sentence for their crimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic. The premise is interesting, it deepens with the interview, and the ending just ties it all together. That realization at the end is great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man what a roller coaster. Really good pacing too, never burying any single part with too much detail and leaving us a new fragment to follow every step along the way. This is superb. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing pacing. This reads in tune of a Nuclear revenge story and I love it +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-4662-1 through -10 aren't under any kind of "misery", they're either dead or in cryogenic storage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really good article. It weaves together nicely, has some great buldup and a good ending. Whole hearted +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. I was confused at first as to what happened to the multiple Billys that kept showing up, but once it was revealed it made a chilling type of sense. I do have one question, though. If one Billy is kept happy and psychologically healthy, how does that override the continued misery of the other instances? Wouldn't it be safer to amnesticize and release them to vetted families just to make sure they don't accidentally continue spawning new Billys themselves? Also, would it not be prudent to sterilize the parents to prevent them from accidentally creating a Billy 2.0 (with improved kung fu reality bending powers)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I remember seeing this all the way back in… conceptual phase, I think? The setup is incredibly chilling and the interview (which I tend to be more skeptical of these days) really drives home the fridge horror. There's just something about the dialogue that seems incredibly unsettling, but more because I can actually envision the parents saying what they did. And it's shiver-inducing.

And I'm glad that you went with the ending you did. RIP other Billys.

+1 from me here, now to think about this instead of sleeping…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! This line really brought the piece down-to-earth for me, and was a great reminder of how cold the Foundation can be:

No amnestic use was authorized.

+1!

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to the following people for critique: Zyn, DrLycus, arii uwu, Ihp, Rounderhouse, root, and AgentMalice.

Image credit: https://www.flickr.com/photos/photogramma1/3657221354
